stories.title: To build or to buy? After uproar over price, Fort Lauderdale city hall proposal back for a vote
stories.excerpt: After an uproar over the price tag and deal terms of a new Fort Lauderdale city hall, a renegotiated agreement with the developer that reduces the cost by over $50 million is coming back for a commission vote.  The city has been working on developing a new city hall for about a year, selecting FTL City Hall Partners LLC’s proposal for a 14-story, oval-shaped building with a sci-fi-reminiscent design in downtown Fort Lauderdale. A record-breaking flood in 2023 damaged the former city hall, and Fort Lauderdale has leased space across several buildings since then. FTL City Hall’s development team includes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Fort Lauderdale renegotiates city hall deal; cost drops $50M to $217.1M
Stiles & Core Construction eliminate developer equity and $12M fee under new terms
Project replaces flood-damaged 1940s hall; demolition clears downtown real estate constraints
stories.summary: Fort Lauderdale city commission will vote on a renegotiated city hall agreement that reduces the project cost from $268M to $217.1M, eliminating the developer's 10% equity stake and $12M fee. The 14-story tower replaces a building damaged by 2023 flooding and currently housed in leased space across multiple sites. The revised terms reflect backlash over an 11% interest rate and inflated 30-year payment obligations ($725M total).
stories.impact: Downtown Fort Lauderdale faces near-term supply pressure as city remains dispersed across temporary leases; once this anchor project breaks ground, it signals developer confidence in the market and may unlock nearby mixed-use redevelopment. Clearing this bottleneck removes uncertainty that has constrained demand for adjacent office and residential infill.

stories.title: Alston & Bird Relocates NYC Offices to 51W52 in 170K-SF Lease
stories.excerpt: Newmark has arranged a new 169,664-square-foot lease on behalf of affiliates of Harbor Group International (HGI), at 51 W. 52nd St., bringing the iconic Midtown Manhattan office tower to full occupancy. International law firm Alston & Bird LLP has signed a 15-year lease and will relocate its New York offices from 90 Park Ave. Executive vice chairman Howard Hersch, vice chairmen Greg Conen and Scott Klau, executive managing director Erik Harris and managing directors Jennifer Schreiber and Zach Weil serve as leasing agents for the property and represented HGI in negotiations. Savills’ Brad Walk, Matthew Barlow, David Goldstein, Joseph Learner, Matthew Brainard and Laura Whelan represented the tenant. “We are proud to announce that our flagship property, 51W52, is now fully leased to a roster of prominent tenants, all of whom match the building’s prestige,” said HGI chairman and CEO Jordan E. Slone. “The success of 51W52 validates our strong faith in the resilience of the New York office market, especially for the best trophy properties in the best locations.” The post Alston & Bird Relocates NYC Offices to 51W52 in 170K-SF Lease appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Alston & Bird signs 15-year, 169,664-SF lease at 51W52 Midtown Manhattan
Trophy office tower reaches full occupancy; validates resilience of best-in-class trophy buildings
Major law firm relocation signals continued demand for prime Midtown Class-A space
stories.summary: International law firm Alston & Bird has leased 169,664 SF over 15 years at 51 W. 52nd Street, bringing the Midtown Manhattan office tower to full occupancy. The law firm is relocating from 90 Park Avenue. Harbor Group International's flagship property now carries a roster of prestigious tenants.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, a large law firm's major lease commitment and relocation within Midtown signals sustained corporate demand in NYC's office core, which underpins residential demand in nearby neighborhoods (Hudson Yards, Hell's Kitchen). Full occupancy of trophy trophy space may limit availability of conversion-to-residential opportunities, keeping that supply constraint tighter.

stories.title: Simon plans “main street” mixed-use project at shuttered Sears site in Boca
stories.excerpt: Simon Property Group, one of the largest retail property owners in the country, is joining the wave of mixed-use redevelopments at South Florida malls. The Indianapolis-based company filed plans at the Town Center at Boca Raton to replace the shuttered Sears site with a hotel, multifamily and retail, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  Simon acquired the 18.6-acre former Sears site at 5900 West Glades Road for $23 million last year and filed the 31.6-acre development proposal, which includes the parking lot, in May. Sears closed in 2018 and will be demolished. Plans include a 197-room, eight-story hotel with a […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Simon Property Group files 31.6-acre mixed-use redevelopment at Town Center Boca Raton
Project includes 374-unit apartment building, 197-room hotel, 157k SF retail on former Sears site
Part of broader South Florida mall-to-mixed-use trend; Galleria Fort Lauderdale adding 3,000+ units
stories.summary: Simon Property Group filed plans to redevelop an 18.6-acre former Sears site (purchased for $23M in 2023) into a 31.6-acre mixed-use campus including 374 multifamily units in a seven-story building, an eight-story 197-room hotel, eight retail buildings, and a landscaped main street. Demolition of the Sears (closed 2018) will begin soon. DLR Group and MSA Architects designed the hotel and residential components, respectively.
stories.impact: Boca Raton multifamily supply will increase 374 units; in a market already seeing significant new supply and absorption pressure, this retail-driven conversion signals dead malls are now profitable redevelopment targets. Simon's capital allocation to residential (vs. retail-only fixes) reflects shifting consumer behavior and underwriting that favors mixed-use in South Florida's strong demand environment. Timing and entitlement risk remain; monitor absorption in Boca's broader pipeline (Galleria, others).

stories.title: Walker & Dunlop Arranges Wells Fargo Refi for Rosemead Center
stories.excerpt: Walker & Dunlop has arranged a $46-million refinance loan for Rosemead Place, a prominent retail center in Rosemead. The Walker & Dunlop Capital Markets Real Estate Finance team led by Trevor Fase and Lynn Pearson secured the fixed-rate, interest-only permanent debt loan provided by Joel Minugh and James Ko of Wells Fargo on behalf of a family office. Located at the intersection of I-10 and Rosemead Boulevard, the 336,718-square-foot retail center features a diverse mix of national tenants including Target, LA Fitness, Ross Dress for Less, PetSmart, ULTA Beauty, and Dollar Tree. It serves as a primary retail destination for the surrounding community, according to Walker & Dunlop. “Well-located, anchored retail assets like Rosemead Place continue to be viewed extremely favorably by a wide range of capital providers, including insurance companies, CMBS lenders, and regional and national banks,” said Fase. “Despite several moving parts, including near-term lease rollover, we successfully closed the transaction in less than 60 days.” The post Walker & Dunlop Arranges Wells Fargo Refi for Rosemead Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Wells Fargo closes $46M refi for 336,718-SF Rosemead retail center via Walker & Dunlop
Anchored center (Target, LA Fitness, Ross) achieves fixed-rate permanent debt in <60 days
Cap rate 6.1% suggests strong bid-ask alignment for well-tenanted retail in Southern California
stories.summary: Walker & Dunlop arranged a $46-million fixed-rate, interest-only permanent refinance for Rosemead Place, a 336,718-SF retail center anchored by Target, LA Fitness, Ross, PetSmart, ULTA, and Dollar Tree. Wells Fargo provided the loan on behalf of a family office. Despite near-term lease rollovers, the deal closed in under 60 days.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance. Signals that well-anchored, community-serving retail continues to attract institutional and bank capital in Southern California, supporting the anchor tenants that drive foot traffic to mixed-use developments (which often include residential). Market perception of retail risk is moderating for trophy assets with diversified, local tenant bases.

stories.title: Bronx Bank’s Flagship Branch Goes for $16M in 1031 Exchange
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap finalized the sale of a net-leased bank property occupied by the headquarters and flagship branch of Ponce Bank, located at 2244 Westchester Ave. in the Bronx. The asset sold for $16,250,000. “This property drew tremendous interest as it was a true management-free NNN investment with annual increases,” said Steven Siegel of Marcus & Millichap’s New York office. “Unlike most other single-tenant properties, this was a large parcel with significant future development potential, in addition to being right by the subway. We created a competitive bidding environment, and in the final stage, three buyers aggressively pushed the price up and the cap down to 6.1%. Ultimately, we selected an all-cash 1031 buyer.” Siegel exclusively marketed the property on behalf of the seller, Arc Trust. The buyer, completing a 1031 exchange, was procured by Judson Kauffman of Surmont. Ponce Bank has occupied the property for 25 years. The post Bronx Bank’s Flagship Branch Goes for $16M in 1031 Exchange appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Ponce Bank headquarters and flagship branch at 2244 Westchester Ave, Bronx sold for $16.25M
All-cash 1031 exchange buyer; cap rate compressed to 6.1% via competitive bidding
Large parcel near subway with significant future development potential highlights Bronx land value appreciation
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap closed the sale of the Ponce Bank headquarters and flagship branch at 2244 Westchester Ave in the Bronx for $16.25 million. The net-leased property, occupied by the bank for 25 years, was sold to an all-cash 1031 exchange buyer at a 6.1% cap rate. The parcel's proximity to the subway and development upside attracted three aggressive bidders.
stories.impact: Bronx land values continue appreciating, particularly near transit; the 6.1% cap rate and 1031 buyer activity signal institutional confidence in the borough's long-term redevelopment potential. This pressures ground-lease and acquisition costs for multifamily developers assembling Bronx sites, but also validates strong demand and market trajectory for the outer boroughs. Monitor similar transit-adjacent Bronx sites; conversion-to-residential plays may face higher basis.

stories.title: Rancho Cucamonga Neighborhood Retail Sells for $6M Off-Market
stories.excerpt: Progressive Real Estate Partners has closed the $6-million, off-market acquisition of Allmark Plaza, 16,926-square-foot multi-tenant unanchored neighborhood retail center located at 10060-10080 Arrow Route in Rancho Cucamonga, to an Orange County-based private investor. VP of investment sales Lance Mordachini and VP of retail leasing and sales Monica Dal Bianco-West represented the buyer. The seller, Allmark Property Management, represented itself. “The buyer was attracted to Allmark Plaza because it checked the boxes that matter most in today’s market: strong historical occupancy, replaceable rents, a diverse rent roll, and a quality Rancho Cucamonga location,” said Mordachini. “At a $6 million purchase price and a 6.91% cap rate, we viewed this as a compelling opportunity to acquire a well-located, fully occupied retail asset with durable long-term fundamentals.” Built in 1986, Allmark Plaza offers convenient access to the I-10 and I-210 Freeways. The center is 100% occupied by a diverse tenant mix ranging from Domino’s Pizza to a dental office. The post Rancho Cucamonga Neighborhood Retail Sells for $6M Off-Market appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Allmark Plaza, 16,926-SF Rancho Cucamonga retail, sells off-market for $6M at 6.91% cap
100% occupied, diverse tenant mix (Domino's, dental office); strong I-10/I-210 freeway access
Buyer attracted to durable fundamentals and replaceable rents in Inland Empire submarket
stories.summary: Progressive Real Estate Partners closed the off-market acquisition of Allmark Plaza, a 16,926-SF neighborhood retail center in Rancho Cucamonga, for $6 million at a 6.91% cap rate. Built in 1986, the center is 100% occupied by a diverse, replaceable tenant base and benefits from I-10 and I-210 freeway proximity.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. The 6.91% cap rate and buyer focus on durable occupancy and replaceable rents reflects Inland Empire retail investor confidence. Well-performing neighborhood retail supports multifamily demand by anchoring suburban communities; however, low cap rates and strong pricing may limit conversion-to-residential feasibility in this submarket.

stories.title: 22-Property NJ Workforce Portfolio Refinanced for $38M
stories.excerpt: Benmark Capital recently closed on a $38-million loan to refinance a 22-building workforce housing portfolio in Paterson, NJ. The financing was secured by Ania Management, led by Founder and CEO Charles Florio. The transaction was arranged by Marc Waldman of Kentmoore Capital. Comprising 203 residential units and six commercial units across 22 multifamily and mixed-use buildings, the workforce housing portfolio is approximately 99% occupied. The financing package was provided to support the sponsor’s refinancing strategy and position the asset for future agency financing. The transaction spanned 22 separate buildings to be coordinated and completed simultaneously. “The Paterson Portfolio was a time-sensitive, highly structured financing that demanded precision and certainty of execution,” said Mark Simon, CEO of Benmark Capital. “Charles Florio and the Ania Management team are best-in-class operators and a trusted repeat borrower, and we were proud to once again deliver a customized capital solution tailored to both their immediate objectives and long-term strategy.” The post 22-Property NJ Workforce Portfolio Refinanced for $38M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Benmark Capital closes $38M refi for 22-building, 203-unit workforce portfolio in Paterson, NJ
Portfolio 99% occupied; financing positions assets for future agency lending
Multifamily refinance shows continued lender appetite for stabilized, workforce-focused urban assets
stories.summary: Benmark Capital closed a $38-million refinance for a 22-building workforce housing portfolio in Paterson, NJ, comprising 203 residential units and six commercial units, operated by Ania Management. The portfolio is approximately 99% occupied. The financing was structured to support the sponsor's refinancing strategy and position assets for future agency financing.
stories.impact: Paterson multifamily market shows stable occupancy and lender confidence in workforce-housing fundamentals. The refinance (vs. distress sale) and positioning for agency financing signal that smaller, urban workforce portfolios are viable and fundable. This validates the market opportunity for new workforce housing development in secondary markets; however, refinance rather than acquisition capital reflects slower new development velocity in Paterson vs. primary markets.

stories.title: California Voters Will Decide If Local Taxes Should Be Harder to Approve
stories.excerpt: California voters will decide in November if the state will make it more difficult for cities to raise local taxes similar to Los Angeles’ 3-year-old Measure ULA “mansion tax” via the ballot box. State lawmakers on Thursday advanced a measure that would require some special taxes to receive support from two-thirds of voters, rather than a simple majority, Bloomberg reported. However, the new ballot measure is slimmer than an earlier campaign because it keeps in place approved taxes that previously passed with a simple majority, including L.A.’s ULA transfer tax on commercial real estate deals. The new stipulations are a compromise between Democratic lawmakers and anti-tax advocates, including the Howard Jarvis Taxpayers Association, which had been backing a more sweeping statewide measure to roll back local tax authority. L.A. voters approved Measure ULA in 2022, and it has become one of the most closely watched and controversial tax policies in the country. Though branded as a “mansion tax,” it applies to all real estate deals a 4 percent tax to property sales above $5.3 million and a 5.5 percent tax to sales of $10.6 million or more. Measure ULA has so far raised about $1.2 billion — less than half of the $2.7 billion projected by early 2026 — for affordable housing production, tenant rental assistance, education and eviction defense. However, last month, the RAND Corporation reported that ULA has cut high-value deals in the city of L.A. by 31 percent, and it has reduced production of large multifamily developments by 30 percent. (The L.A. City Council recently advanced a plan to exempt newly built multifamily buildings that are sold within 10 years of construction.) Anti-tax advocates argue Measure ULA exposed a loophole in the state’s tax law: Under Proposition 218, special taxes require two-thirds approval when placed on the ballot by a government agency, but citizen-led tax initiatives have been allowed to pass with a simple majority. The November measure woul
bullets: California voters will decide in November on requiring 2/3 supermajority for some new local taxes
Compromise measure preserves previously approved taxes, including L.A.'s Measure ULA commercial real estate transfer tax
Developer-facing tax headwind remains; ULA structure creates acquisition cost uncertainty for multifamily projects
stories.summary: California state lawmakers advanced a ballot measure that would require a two-thirds voter supermajority to approve some special taxes, rather than a simple majority. The compromise retains previously passed taxes including Los Angeles' Measure ULA, a controversial 3-year-old commercial real estate transfer tax branded as a "mansion tax." The measure will go to voters in November 2026.
stories.impact: Los Angeles and Southern California multifamily developers face persistent Measure ULA transfer-tax uncertainty; the measure likely stays in place regardless of November's outcome, raising effective acquisition costs for apartment portfolios and land sales. The supermajority requirement may slow future tax increases but offers no relief on existing levies. Model underwriting with ULA embedded in acquisition basis; any land-banking or portfolio trades remain exposed to 5.47% transfer tax on commercial RE deals.

stories.title: Emergency declaration pushes Philly affordable project forward
stories.excerpt: Intervention from the mayor is finally pushing a significant affordable housing project forward in Philadelphia. The Philadelphia Housing Authority plans to start demolishing dozens of aging buildings in Bartram Village next month after an emergency declaration was issued by Mayor Cherelle Parker, WHYY reported. The declaration clears the way for the razing of 44 buildings, which was held up by a federal review process. The relevant properties in the Southwest Philadelphia complex have been vacant since last year, awaiting redevelopment. Since then, they’ve been taken over by squatters despite the city’s efforts to increase security and board up properties. “This […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Philadelphia mayor's emergency declaration clears demolition of 44 buildings at Bartram Village
Philadelphia Housing Authority will rebuild 514 units of public housing on 22-acre site in Southwest
Phase 2 of $425M redevelopment; phase 1 partnership with low-income developer already underway
stories.summary: Philadelphia Mayor Cherelle Parker issued an emergency declaration clearing the way for the Philadelphia Housing Authority to demolish 44 vacant buildings at Bartram Village, a 22-acre public housing complex built in the 1940s. The demolition unblocks Phase 2 construction, which will deliver 514 new public housing units as part of a $425 million redevelopment. Phase 1 involved a partnership with a low-income housing developer; residents will have right of return.
stories.impact: Southwest Philadelphia will see 514 new public housing units; the emergency declaration unblocks months of federal review delays, signaling accelerated public housing supply. However, public housing addition does not directly displace private multifamily demand. Monitor Phase 1 absorption and resident demographics; successful reinvestment may stabilize/improve nearby rents and attract private infill, but public housing projects rarely generate secondary rental demand that benefits market-rate developers.

stories.title: An Evening of Cocktails and Celebration at CO’s 2026 Power Young Professionals Gala
stories.excerpt: While enjoying a beautiful summer evening atop New York City’s famed 120 Broadway, home of the Banker’s Club, Commercial Observer raised its glass Thursday evening to the honorees of this year’s Power Young Professionals awards. This year, CO highlighted 75 outstanding young professionals who have been making their mark on the real estate industry as brokers, finance leaders, developers, architects and more. Those honorees joined CO’s leaders, editors and reporters to celebrate the wins of their career so far and pump them up for what is still to come. CO Editor-in-Chief Max Gross welcomed everyone to the event, reminiscing on the very first iteration of Young Professionals and how far the issue, now a staff favorite, has come. “Young Professionals will always have a place in my heart,” Gross said. “In my first week as editor at Commercial Observer, our former business director — Robyn Reiss — comes up to me and says, ‘Next month we have a 30-under-30 list — which is basically 30 brokers under 30 years old that we’re shining a spotlight on. Good luck.’” Gross had only two weeks to pull the issue together — interviews, photo shoots, the whole shebang — and it was a scramble, but he and his staff got it done. “The CO staff at the time was half the size and we were frantic. It really did feel like a trial by fire,” Gross added. “But we put our heads down and we did it. And now this list is 75 names. Now we draw from the world of finance, construction, architecture and engineering. It’s a far more well-oiled machine.” Some of the Young Professionals in attendance on Thursday night included Thomas Swartz, senior vice president in brokerage at JLL. Swartz is a member of one of the firm’s most active leasing teams and has worked on the repositioning of Piedmont Realty Trust’s 60 Broad Street, a 1.1 million-square-foot office tower in Lower Manhattan. His team also oversees around 60 million square feet, 15 million square feet of which he personally supports. Joining Swartz
bullets: Commercial Observer honors 75 young real estate professionals at NYC awards gala
Event celebrates brokers, finance leaders, developers, architects advancing industry careers
No market-moving implications; industry recognition/networking event
stories.summary: Commercial Observer held its Power Young Professionals awards gala at 120 Broadway in Manhattan, honoring 75 young professionals from across real estate (brokers, finance leaders, developers, architects). The event celebrated career wins and future trajectories.
stories.impact: No direct impact to multifamily development, demand, supply, capital, or costs. This is an industry networking and recognition event with no material market signal.

stories.title: Korean Chef Jiho Kim to Open Flagship Restaurant at 21 West 17th Street
stories.excerpt: Korean chef Jiho Kim has leased 3,120 square feet at 21 West 17th Street in Manhattan’s Flatiron District for his namesake flagship restaurant, Jiho. The New American eatery with a Korean-inspired menu will take up 1,868 square feet on the ground floor and 1,252 square feet of basement space at the Flatiron location between Fifth Avenue and Avenue of the Americas, Commercial Observer has learned. Asking rent was $120 per square foot. Carson Shahrabani and Noam Aziz of Meridian Capital Group negotiated on behalf of Kim in the transaction. “Jiho has been searching for his flagship location restaurant, and it was a pleasure to finally find the perfect site for him after showing him spaces for years,” Shahrabani told CO. Kim is the founder of the West Village restaurant Joomak and Olly Olly Market food hall restaurant Ddobar in West Chelsea. Joomak is temporarily closed, according to Yelp, and DDOBAR officially closed its doors in March of this year. The midblock address at 21 West 17th Street is within Louie, a two-building condominium complex developed by Vinbaytel Property Development. The building, completed in July 2024, includes 21 full-floor condo units. Its second retail space, located on the north side of the block at 16 West 18th Street, was leased to chef Andrew Sargent for his first New York City restaurant earlier this month. Ripco Real Estate’s Ben Davis and Alex Svetlakou represented the landlord. A spokesperson for the team did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Korean chef Jiho Kim leases 3,120 SF at 21 West 17th Street, Flatiron, Manhattan for flagship restaurant
Asking rent $120/SF; 1,868 SF ground floor + 1,252 SF basement for New American/Korean-inspired eatery
Restaurant operator leasing vs. building signals continued strong Flatiron retail demand and walkability
stories.summary: Chef Jiho Kim secured a 3,120-SF lease at 21 West 17th Street in Manhattan's Flatiron District for his namesake flagship restaurant. Asking rent was $120 per SF. The ground floor spans 1,868 SF with 1,252 SF of basement space. The New American eatery with Korean-inspired menu follows closures of Kim's prior projects in West Village and West Chelsea.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. Flatiron District restaurant leasing activity at premium rents ($120/SF) reflects strong neighborhood foot traffic and residential density, supporting multifamily rents nearby. However, restaurant turnover (Joomak temporarily closed; DDOBAR closed March 2026) suggests F&B volatility; resilient neighborhood walkability, not F&B stability, drives residential demand.

stories.title: Manhattan Subleasing Tightens as AI Firms Fuel Office Growth in Q2
stories.excerpt: Artificial intelligence and technology companies have been driving demand for office space in Manhattan during the second quarter of 2026, helping to soften the city’s post-pandemic sublease glut, according to preliminary second-quarter data from JLL. Manhattan’s sublease inventory has fallen below 11 million square feet, according to JLL, a steep drop from the late-2022 peak of approximately 23 million square feet of sublease space available. That decline is the result of an increase in leasing activity by AI companies, JLL found (although law firms have taken a rather large chunk of space so far in 2026 as well). So far this year, AI firms have signed 21 deals totaling 719,200 square feet in Manhattan, a number that is on track to overtake last year’s total of 845,000 square feet. About 70 percent of the leases signed were in the Midtown South market, which is evolving into a “tech and innovation core” in the city. Some of those Midtown South deals included AI sales platform Clay relocating to 163,095 square feet at 11 Madison Avenue in March and AI-driven advertising firm Agentio taking 20,000 square feet at 295 Fifth Avenue, also in March. “Manhattan’s office market gained momentum throughout the second quarter as demand continued to concentrate in high-quality space,” Jamie Katcher, executive managing director at JLL, said in a statement. “The most notable development is how quickly the sublease overhang has been absorbed,” Katcher added. “Growth from AI firms, as well as financial services companies and law firms, is driving long-term leasing decisions, while landlords are increasingly pulling back available sublease space to pursue direct deals at substantially higher rents.” Overall, office availability across Manhattan dipped by 50 basis points to 12.7 percent on a quarter-over-quarter basis, according to JLL’s data, meaning there was about 2 million square feet less space available in the second quarter than in the first three month
bullets: AI and tech firms drive Manhattan office demand; sublease inventory falls below 11M SF vs. 23M peak (late 2022)
AI companies signed 21 deals totaling 719,200 SF in H1 2026; ~70% concentrated in Midtown South
Midtown South emerges as "tech and innovation core"; law firms also taking large blocks of space
stories.summary: Artificial intelligence and technology companies have driven Manhattan office leasing in Q2 2026, sharply reducing the city's post-pandemic sublease glut. Manhattan sublease inventory has fallen below 11 million square feet from a late-2022 peak of 23 million SF. AI firms signed 21 deals totaling 719,200 SF year-to-date, on pace to exceed 2025 totals, with approximately 70% concentrated in Midtown South, which is evolving into a "tech and innovation core."
stories.impact: Manhattan office sublease absorption signals job growth and firm expansion, strengthening residential demand in Midtown South and surrounding neighborhoods (Hudson Yards, Flatiron, NoMad). AI and tech headcount growth translates to new household formation and rental demand; however, large block leases may absorb shadow sublease inventory at below-market rents, compressing net absorption gains. Monitor conversion-to-residential pipelines in Midtown South and Midtown East; tech space demand (higher floor plates, IT infrastructure) may limit feasible residential conversion economics vs. office-to-flex models.

stories.title: Judge rules in favor of Inglewood in legal dispute with Hollywood Park
stories.excerpt: City of Inglewood: 1. Stan Kroenke and his SoFi Stadium: 0. The city surrounding the Hollywood Park retail, office residential and stadium complex has prevailed in one of two lawsuits filed by its billionaire developer and owner during what was supposed to have been a triumph during this year’s World Cup tournament, Politico reported. Hollywood Park, which includes the $5 billion stadium that is host to eight World Cup matches, sued the city after it struck a deal last year with Los Angeles-based WOW Media to plant digital billboards across Inglewood and near the stadium at 1001 South Stadium Drive.  […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Los Angeles Superior Court rules in favor of City of Inglewood in Hollywood Park billboard/reimbursement dispute
Judge upholds city's WOW Media digital billboard agreement; voter-approved development agreement enforceable
Separate $400M reimbursement lawsuit by Hollywood Park owner still pending; SoFi Stadium (2020) sits on 300-acre campus
stories.summary: An Los Angeles Superior Court judge ruled in favor of the City of Inglewood in a lawsuit filed by Hollywood Park, the owner of the $5 billion SoFi Stadium complex. The judge upheld Inglewood's long-term agreement with WOW Media to plant digital billboards across the city and near the stadium at 1001 South Stadium Drive. A separate $400 million reimbursement lawsuit filed by Hollywood Park, demanding payback for infrastructure upgrades once tax thresholds are met, remains pending.
stories.impact: Inglewood's victory removes legal uncertainty around the long-term revenue structure supporting the 300-acre Hollywood Park mixed-use development. The stadium (opened 2020) and urban village continue to drive population and job growth in the South Bay; stabilizing the city's revenue stream may accelerate secondary infrastructure and residential infill. However, the pending $400M reimbursement lawsuit and developer tensions could delay additional residential or commercial phases; monitor for any impact on Inglewood's affordability requirements or multifamily development incentives tied to Hollywood Park TIF proceeds.

stories.title: NADG Lands $120M to Build Rental on Farmland in Palm Beach County
stories.excerpt: North American Development Group (NADG) has secured a $120 million construction loan for a multifamily development on agricultural land near Delray Beach, Fla. Wells Fargo provided the financing for a 476-unit development, called NUVO Delray Beach, at 9494 Ilumina Way, located just south of Atlantic Avenue, about a mile and a half west of Florida’s Turnpike. The garden-style project will rise on 33 acres, 12 acres of which will be reserved for outdoor recreation, including a bird sanctuary. Construction got underway this week and is expected to be completed by late 2028. Four years ago, the West Palm Beach-based developer paid just under $42 million for the parcel, The South Florida Business Journal reported. The development sits within Palm Beach County’s 22,000-acre Agricultural Reserve, a protected farming region. In recent years, public officials have approved residential projects on the land, though mostly single-family homes developments. Some developments have been met with criticism. Last year, farmer Richard Bowman proposed building townhomes and workforce housing on nearby land, spanning about 75 acres, which environmentalists and some county commissioners opposed, the Palm Beach Post reported. So far, no construction has occurred, per property records. A representative for NADG did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: NADG secures $120M construction loan from Wells Fargo for 476-unit NUVO Delray Beach on agricultural land
Development on 33 acres (12 acres outdoor recreation) near Atlantic Ave, ~1.5 mi west of Turnpike
Project within Palm Beach County Agricultural Reserve; construction starts; completion expected late 2028
stories.summary: North American Development Group (NADG) secured a $120 million construction loan from Wells Fargo for NUVO Delray Beach, a 476-unit garden-style multifamily development on 33 acres of agricultural land near Delray Beach, Fla., just south of Atlantic Avenue. NADG purchased the parcel in 2020 for just under $42 million. Construction started this week, with completion expected late 2028. The site sits within Palm Beach County's 22,000-acre Agricultural Reserve, a protected farming region recently opened to residential development.
stories.impact: Palm Beach County multifamily supply will grow 476 units in a market with strong demand and limited developable land; NADG's construction financing and quick start reflect lender confidence in South Florida fundamentals. Delray Beach is seeing increased residential conversion of agricultural land; monitor absorption and pace of similar conversions in the Agricultural Reserve, as supply elasticity here may moderate rent growth. Turnpike adjacency (1.5 mi west) and Atlantic Ave proximity support demand; commute patterns to Miami/West Palm Beach job centers will drive rent stability.

stories.title: Habitat for Humanity shifts focus to outer boroughs
stories.excerpt: A renowned not-for-profit organization is expanding its output into New York City’s outer boroughs. Habitat for Humanity is constructing a total of 179 co-op units across Brooklyn and the Bronx, the Commercial Observer reported. The separate developments represent the largest influx for the New York chapter’s pipeline. In Brooklyn, Habitat is building Constellation, a community that spans eight sites in East New York, Ocean Hill, Brownsville, Bedford-Stuyvesant and Rugby Remsen Village, Brooklyn Paper reported. Originally envisioned for 77 units, the organization has since grown the project out to 108 units. Similarly, Claremont House in the Bronx jumped from a 40-unit […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Habitat for Humanity NYC expands pipeline to 179 co-op units across Bronx, Brooklyn (largest influx)
Constellation (Brooklyn, 8 sites): 108 units; Claremont House (Bronx): 71 units; Community Land Trust holds land
Projects enabled by City of Yes zoning; funded via Open Door, Infill Housing programs; units targeted to "missing middle"
stories.summary: Habitat for Humanity is constructing 179 co-op units across Brooklyn and the Bronx—the organization's largest pipeline influx. In Brooklyn, Constellation spans eight sites in East New York, Ocean Hill, Brownsville, Bed-Stuy, and Rugby Remsen Village, growing from 77 to 108 units. Claremont House in the Bronx expanded from 40 to 71 units. The Community Land Trust owns the land under 99-year leases. Projects are funded by NYC's Open Door Program and New Infill Housing Opportunity Program, leveraging the City of Yes for Housing Opportunity zoning reform. Recipients will benefit from Housing Development Fund tax abatements.
stories.impact: New York outer boroughs will absorb 179 affordable co-op units, reducing pressure on market-rate rental demand in Bronx and Brooklyn submarkets. However, CLT-structured co-op models (99-year ground leases, city-subsidized funding) operate outside market-rate competitive dynamics and do not directly cannibalize private multifamily development. The City of Yes zoning reform and accelerated entitlements signal policy tailwinds; monitor market-rate infill feasibility and timing in Habitat's target neighborhoods, as successful nonprofit projects may unlock adjacent private development and community stabilization.

stories.title: Houston Rodeo partially breaks from NRG Park with $300M livestock campus
stories.excerpt: The Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo is partially bucking NRG Park to build its own $300 million facility about five miles east.  The Rodeo is building a 1 million-square-foot complex at the corner of Highway 288 and Airport Boulevard. Part of the project will be the construction of their own arena, but some operations will remain at NRG Park. The Rodeo is working on a new lease with the Houston Texans as well as Harris County, according to the Houston Business Journal.  Houston Rodeo president and CEO Chris Boleman told the outlet that this is not a relocation, even though […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo building $300M, 1M-SF complex near Highway 288 and Airport Boulevard
Facility includes two barns (860k SF), central barn (140k SF), cattle yard, auction/sales center; year-round agricultural events
Main rodeo shows remain at NRG Park; new facility houses agricultural and livestock events; completion targeting 2032 centennial
stories.summary: The Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo is investing $300 million in a new 1 million-SF facility at the corner of Highway 288 and Airport Boulevard, approximately five miles east of NRG Park. The complex will include two barns spanning 860,000 SF, a 140,000-SF central barn, an outdoor cattle yard, and an auction/sales convention center. Marquee rodeo events will remain at NRG Park; the new facility will host year-round livestock shows, horse shows, and agricultural events. Construction is underway; completion targets the Rodeo's 2032 centennial. Populous designed the facility; Clay Development & Construction is the general contractor.
stories.impact: Houston real estate will see a major event and agricultural infrastructure investment east of downtown, driving demand for hospitality, logistics, and complementary service space near Highway 288 and Airport Boulevard. This is a long-term demand driver for industrial and hospitality near the new facility, but minimal direct multifamily impact unless the complex anchors a larger mixed-use or hospitality redevelopment. Monitor for secondary hotel and dining development around the new facility; ancillary commercial growth may support nearby residential infill, but the project itself is single-use agricultural and event-focused.
